text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac widely and begins dissection along the lower border of the pancreas and the large cystic mass. The mass, identified as a mucinocystic tumor, is adherent to the transverse muscle column, proximal jejunal loops, and duodenal flexure, which are carefully dissected off. A portion of the mesocolon is left attached to the cyst to prevent rupture and spillage. Dissection continues inferiorly towards the retroperitoneum, identifying the loose areolar tissue plate.

The surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric vein, portal vein, and splenic vein during medial dissection. The junction of the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein is visible. The lesser sac is further opened towards the duodenum to enhance the surgical field visibility.

The surgeon carefully dissects behind the neck of the pancreas, addressing the stretched and arched splenic vein due to the cyst size. Small tributaries draining into the vein are clipped, and the splenic vein is divided. The short stem of the splenic vein with the portal vein is clipped and divided. The neck of the pancreas is divided to facilitate identification and dissection of the splenic artery, which is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the body and tail of the pancreas in the retroperitoneum, carefully separating the spleen and associated structures. The short gastric vessels are divided, and the entire specimen is placed into a retrieval bag. Fine sutures are applied to secure the specimen for removal.
